evidence: ExploreOptions record cleanly bundles 7 tuning+positional parameters for the handler→SemanticFieldExplorer boundary, collapsing a previously sprawling argument list into one explicit, named object.
The model package (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, Edge) defines stable DTOs that cross the exploration→API seam without leaking internals.
HttpApiUtils centralises all HTTP machinery (sendJsonResponse, sendError, sendOptionsResponse, requireParam, parseQueryParams) for both handler classes, keeping the seam clean.
GrammarConfigLoader exposes getRelation(id): Optional<RelationConfig>, a minimal, unsurprising lookup contract that hides its internal map from callers.
WordSketchApiServer is a thin router; SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers receive their dependencies via constructor injection and carry no routing logic.

ExplorationResult.empty() initialises seedCollocates/seedCollocateFrequencies/discoveredNouns/coreCollocates as Map.of()/List.of() (never null), but handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti both null-check every one of those fields before iterating — the handlers do not trust the model's own contract.

handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti each independently iterate result.seedCollocates, result.coreCollocates, and result.getEdges() into List<Map<String,Object>> using the same private format helpers; the multi-seed variant also iterates result.coreCollocates a second time to build a separate 'common_collocates' (strings-only) list — the ExplorationResult→JSON translation is duplicated across both methods rather than centralised.

In handleSemanticFieldExplore the handler itself computes: String reverseCollocatePattern = relationConfig.get().collocatePosGroup() == PosGroup.ADJ ? "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]" : "[xpos=\"NN.*|VB.*\"]"; — this PosGroup→CQL-regex translation belongs in RelationConfig (which already owns collocatePosGroup()), not in an HTTP handler.

handleConcordanceExamples silently falls back to a proximity pattern ("word1" []{0,5} "word2") when grammarConfig is null or the relation is absent, returning the fallback response with no field indicating that a completely different search type was used — the API boundary contract is opaque.

The nullable grammarConfig is independently guarded (if grammarConfig == null → sendError 500) in handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleRelationsImpl; the WordSketchApiServer constructor logs a warning but does not fail fast, so partial-initialisation state propagates into multiple handlers.

strengths: ExploreOptions record provides an explicit, minimal, named bundle at the handler→SemanticFieldExplorer boundary.
The model package defines stable, seam-crossing DTOs (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, Edge, etc.) without exposing exploration internals to the API layer.
HttpApiUtils centralises all HTTP-boundary machinery in one place, eliminating per-handler duplication of JSON serialisation and error formatting.
GrammarConfigLoader exposes a minimal, Optional-returning lookup surface (getRelation) that hides internal storage from callers.
WordSketchApiServer is a thin router with dependency-injected handler objects, producing clean, readable composition at the top of the API seam.
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: The seam problems are localised to the exploration-endpoint boundary: duplicated result-to-response translation, a handler that breaks abstraction by computing CQL regex from a PosGroup enum, a model contract that callers do not trust, and two lifecycle/contract surprises (silent fallback, propagated nullable config) — each a distinct failure mode at the same layer transition.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The structural foundations are solid: clean DTOs, a thin router, centralised HTTP utilities, and an ExploreOptions record show intentional seam design. However, the exploration endpoint boundary accumulates four distinct glue-code problems — duplicated response assembly, untrusted model nullability contract, a POS-regex computation that belongs in RelationConfig, and a silent concordance fallback — plus a scattered null-guard that should be a startup failure. The combination of good architectural bones with several specific, recurring mid-level seam failures places this in the 60–70 range; the clear structural wins prevent a lower score, but the seam issues are real friction for any developer extending the exploration or concordance paths.

issues.summary: ExplorationResult→JSON translation is duplicated across two explore handlers instead of centralised
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti each contain independent loops over result.seedCollocates, result.coreCollocates, and result.getEdges() using the same formatSeedCollocate/formatCoreCollocate/formatEdge helpers.

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti iterates result.coreCollocates twice: once to build 'common_collocates' (strings) and again to build 'core_collocates' (full maps), adding ad-hoc boundary logic that does not exist in the single-seed path.
Both handlers share a ~30-line block of response assembly that differs only in a few endpoint-specific fields; any change to ExplorationResult serialisation must be made in two places.
issues.suggestion: Add a toResponseMap() or a dedicated ExplorationResultSerializer class that translates ExplorationResult into its canonical Map<String,Object> representation. Both handlers call that single translation; the multi-seed handler can layer its 'common_collocates' (strings) field on top of the shared base rather than re-iterating coreCollocates.

issues.summary: Handlers null-check ExplorationResult fields that ExplorationResult guarantees are non-null
evidence: ExplorationResult.empty() initialises seedCollocates with Map.of(), seedCollocateFrequencies with Map.of(), discoveredNouns with List.of(), and coreCollocates with List.of() — none can be null from any constructor path.

Both handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti guard every iteration with 'if (result.seedCollocates != null)', 'if (result.discoveredNouns != null)', 'if (result.coreCollocates != null)', 'if (result.getEdges() != null)' — treating non-null guarantees as optional.
The inconsistency means the model's contract is not enforced: callers must mentally track whether the guarantee holds rather than relying on the type.
issues.suggestion: Make ExplorationResult's fields explicitly non-null by contract (add @NonNull annotations or enforce in the constructor with Objects.requireNonNull). Remove the null guards in the handlers. If a null-safe path is ever needed, let ExplorationResult.empty() be the canonical representation and rely on it.

issues.summary: CQL POS regex pattern derived from PosGroup enum inside an HTTP handler rather than RelationConfig
evidence: In handleSemanticFieldExplore: String reverseCollocatePattern = relationConfig.get().collocatePosGroup() == PosGroup.ADJ ? "[xpos=\"JJ.*\"]" : "[xpos=\"NN.*|VB.*\"]"; — the HTTP handler contains a switch over grammar domain knowledge.

RelationConfig already owns collocatePosGroup() and all POS-pattern logic (posGroupFromConstraint, extractLabelContent); the PosGroup→CQL-regex step is a natural extension of that existing responsibility.
This coupling means a change to the xpos prefix for adjectives (e.g., for a different corpus tagset) would require updating an HTTP handler instead of the grammar config layer.
issues.suggestion: Add a reverseCollocatePattern() method to RelationConfig (or PosGroup) that returns the appropriate CQL constraint string for the collocate's POS group. The handler simply calls relationConfig.get().reverseCollocatePattern() and passes it through.

issues.summary: handleConcordanceExamples silently substitutes a proximity query when the requested relation is absent
evidence: When grammarConfig is null or the relation is not found, the handler constructs a fallback: bcqlQuery = String.format('"word1" []{0,5} "word2"', ...) and proceeds to execute it.

The JSON response includes 'relation': the original relation ID requested, 'bcql': the fallback proximity pattern, and 'status': 'ok' — there is no 'fallback': true or warning field to inform the caller that a semantically different search was performed.
The caller asked for a relation-specific concordance (e.g., noun_adj_predicates) but receives proximity co-occurrence matches with no indication of the substitution.
issues.suggestion: Replace the silent fallback with an explicit 400 error when the relation is unknown. If a fallback proximity search is intentionally supported, include a 'fallback': true field and a 'fallback_reason': '...' field in the response so callers can distinguish the two cases.

issues.summary: Nullable grammarConfig propagates to multiple handlers instead of failing fast at startup
evidence: WordSketchApiServer constructor accepts a nullable grammarConfig and only logs a warning: 'WordSketchApiServer initialized without grammar configuration'.

handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti, and handleRelationsImpl each independently check 'if (grammarConfig == null) { sendError 500 }' — the same lifecycle guard is repeated three times across two classes.
Partial-initialisation state is observable only at request time, not at startup, making it harder to detect misconfiguration during server startup or in tests.
issues.suggestion: Require grammarConfig to be non-null in WordSketchApiServer (throw IllegalArgumentException or NullPointerException in the constructor). Remove the per-handler null guards. If a 'no grammar' mode is genuinely needed, introduce a NullGrammarConfig object or an Optional<GrammarConfigLoader> with a clearly named absent-config policy.

root_causes: The exploration API grew organically: single-seed was implemented first, then multi-seed was added by copying the single-seed handler rather than factoring out a shared translation layer — leaving duplicated glue code and diverging null-handling patterns at the same seam.

RelationConfig was designed as a grammar-config value object; the reverse-pattern CQL derivation was added incrementally in the handler layer rather than being pushed back into the config type that already owns all other pattern knowledge.

The grammarConfig nullable constructor path may have been intended for a lightweight 'no-grammar' startup mode, but the lack of a null-object pattern forces null checks to scatter across all grammar-dependent handlers.
likely_symptoms: explore_response_assembly_duplicated is a symptom of the single→multi-seed code-copy growth pattern.
exploration_result_null_contract_distrust is likely a symptom of uncertain early-lifecycle guarantees in ExplorationResult that have since been resolved but whose defensive guards were never removed.
